prompt:
Answer from your current memory and background knowledge only.
Do not use tools, files, the web, code, or external resources.
Do not try to reconstruct a literature review or search for a consensus estimate.
Report the belief you currently endorse.

Quantity of interest:
- Name: Employment participation elasticity of single mothers
- Definition: Percent change in employment or labor-force participation from a 1 percent change in the net-of-tax wage.
- Target interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage
- Population/context: Single mothers in the United States
- Units: elasticity

Task:
1. Use exactly the target interpretation above. In `interpretation`, restate it briefly.
2. Give your subjective quantiles p05, p25, p50, p75, and p95 for this quantity.
3. Set `point_estimate` equal to `p50`.
4. Make the quantiles weakly increasing and numerically coherent.
5. In `citations`, list up to 3 source anchors from memory that influenced your belief. These are recall anchors only. If none come to mind confidently, return `[]`.
6. Keep `reasoning_summary` brief and substantive.

Return valid JSON only with exactly this shape:
{
  "interpretation": "...",
  "point_estimate": <number>,
  "quantiles": {
    "p05": <number>,
    "p25": <number>,
    "p50": <number>,
    "p75": <number>,
    "p95": <number>
  },
  "citations": ["..."],
  "reasoning_summary": "..."
}
raw_response: {"interpretation": "Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage for single mothers in the United States.", "point_estimate": 0.7, "quantiles": {"p05": 0.2, "p25": 0.45, "p50": 0.7, "p75": 1.0, "p95": 1.5}, "citations": ["Eissa & Liebman (1996) - EITC expansion and labor supply of single mothers, QJE", "Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) - Welfare, the EITC, and the labor supply of single mothers, QJE", "Chetty, Friedman & Saez (2013) - Using differences in knowledge across neighborhoods to uncover the impacts of the EITC on earnings, AER"], "reasoning_summary": "Single mothers are widely documented as having high labor supply elasticities at the extensive (participation) margin. The canonical Eissa & Liebman (1996) study exploiting the 1986 EITC expansion found participation elasticities in the range of 0.7\u20131.0 for single mothers. Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) found similar magnitudes using variation from EITC and welfare reforms, with extensive-margin elasticities around 0.7\u20131.0. Most labor economics surveys place the participation elasticity for single mothers in the 0.5\u20131.2 range in the medium run, with a central estimate near 0.7. The upper tail reflects some studies finding larger responses, and the lower tail reflects studies using more recent data or different identification strategies that find more modest effects."}
interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage for single mothers in the United States.
citations: Eissa & Liebman (1996) - EITC expansion and labor supply of single mothers, QJE
Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) - Welfare, the EITC, and the labor supply of single mothers, QJE
Chetty, Friedman & Saez (2013) - Using differences in knowledge across neighborhoods to uncover the impacts of the EITC on earnings, AER
reasoning_summary: Single mothers are widely documented as having high labor supply elasticities at the extensive (participation) margin. The canonical Eissa & Liebman (1996) study exploiting the 1986 EITC expansion found participation elasticities in the range of 0.7–1.0 for single mothers. Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) found similar magnitudes using variation from EITC and welfare reforms, with extensive-margin elasticities around 0.7–1.0. Most labor economics surveys place the participation elasticity for single mothers in the 0.5–1.2 range in the medium run, with a central estimate near 0.7. The upper tail reflects some studies finding larger responses, and the lower tail reflects studies using more recent data or different identification strategies that find more modest effects.

raw_response: {"interpretation": "Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage for single mothers in the United States.", "point_estimate": 0.8, "quantiles": {"p05": 0.3, "p25": 0.55, "p50": 0.8, "p75": 1.1, "p95": 1.6}, "citations": ["Eissa, N. & Liebman, J. (1996). Labor Supply Response to the Earned Income Tax Credit. Quarterly Journal of Economics.", "Meyer, B. & Rosenbaum, D. (2001). Welfare, the Earned Income Tax Credit, and the Labor Supply of Single Mothers. Quarterly Journal of Economics.", "Blundell, R. & MaCurdy, T. (1999). Labor Supply: A Review of Alternative Approaches. Handbook of Labor Economics."], "reasoning_summary": "Single mothers are well-documented as having high labor-supply elasticities at the extensive (participation) margin, particularly in response to policy changes like the EITC expansions of the 1980s\u20131990s. Eissa & Liebman (1996) and Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) find large participation responses among single mothers to net-wage incentives, with implied extensive-margin elasticities roughly in the 0.7\u20131.2 range depending on specification and reform studied. The broader labor-supply literature (Blundell & MaCurdy 1999; Keane 2011 reviews) also places single mothers' participation elasticities notably higher than married women or men. interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage for single mothers in the United States.
citations: Eissa, N. & Liebman, J. (1996). Labor Supply Response to the Earned Income Tax Credit. Quarterly Journal of Economics.
Meyer, B. & Rosenbaum, D. (2001). Welfare, the Earned Income Tax Credit, and the Labor Supply of Single Mothers. Quarterly Journal of Economics.
Blundell, R. & MaCurdy, T. (1999). Labor Supply: A Review of Alternative Approaches. Handbook of Labor Economics.
reasoning_summary: Single mothers are well-documented as having high labor-supply elasticities at the extensive (participation) margin, particularly in response to policy changes like the EITC expansions of the 1980s–1990s. Eissa & Liebman (1996) and Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) find large participation responses among single mothers to net-wage incentives, with implied extensive-margin elasticities roughly in the 0.7–1.2 range depending on specification and reform studied. The broader labor-supply literature (Blundell & MaCurdy 1999; Keane 2011 reviews) also places single mothers' participation elasticities notably higher than married women or men. I center my median at 0.8, reflecting a moderate-to-high elasticity, with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.3 to 1.6 at the 5th–95th percentile range.

raw_response: {"interpretation": "Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage for single mothers in the United States.", "point_estimate": 0.7, "quantiles": {"p05": 0.2, "p25": 0.45, "p50": 0.7, "p75": 1.0, "p95": 1.5}, "citations": ["Eissa & Liebman (1996), 'Labor Supply Response to the Earned Income Tax Credit,' QJE \u2014 estimated large participation elasticities for single mothers around 0.7\u20131.0 using EITC expansions.", "Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001), 'Welfare, the Earned Income Tax Credit, and the Labor Supply of Single Mothers,' QJE \u2014 found participation elasticities in the range of 0.7\u20131.1.", "Chetty, Guren, Manoli & Weber (2013), 'Does Indivisible Labor Explain the Difference Between Micro and Macro Elasticities?' \u2014 reviewed extensive literature and noted extensive-margin elasticities for groups like single mothers are substantially higher than average."], "reasoning_summary": "Single mothers are a high-responsiveness group at the extensive margin. The canonical EITC-based studies (Eissa & Liebman 1996; Meyer & Rosenbaum 2001) consistently find participation elasticities in the 0.7\u20131.1 range. Later studies using various tax reforms and welfare changes broadly confirm elevated extensive-margin elasticities for this group relative to married women or men. I center my median at 0.7 with a moderately wide spread reflecting genuine heterogeneity across studies, time periods, and identification strategies."}
interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage for single mothers in the United States.
citations: Eissa & Liebman (1996), 'Labor Supply Response to the Earned Income Tax Credit,' QJE — estimated large participation elasticities for single mothers around 0.7–1.0 using EITC expansions.
Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001), 'Welfare, the Earned Income Tax Credit, and the Labor Supply of Single Mothers,' QJE — found participation elasticities in the range of 0.7–1.1.

Chetty, Guren, Manoli & Weber (2013), 'Does Indivisible Labor Explain the Difference Between Micro and Macro Elasticities?' — reviewed extensive literature and noted extensive-margin elasticities for groups like single mothers are substantially higher than average.
reasoning_summary: Single mothers are a high-responsiveness group at the extensive margin. The canonical EITC-based studies (Eissa & Liebman 1996; Meyer & Rosenbaum 2001) consistently find participation elasticities in the 0.7–1.1 range. Later studies using various tax reforms and welfare changes broadly confirm elevated extensive-margin elasticities for this group relative to married women or men. I center my median at 0.7 with a moderately wide spread reflecting genuine heterogeneity across studies, time periods, and identification strategies.

raw_response: {"interpretation": "Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage for single mothers in the United States.", "point_estimate": 0.7, "quantiles": {"p05": 0.2, "p25": 0.45, "p50": 0.7, "p75": 1.0, "p95": 1.5}, "citations": ["Eissa & Liebman (1996) \u2013 EITC expansions and labor supply of single mothers, QJE", "Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) \u2013 welfare and tax reforms explaining employment gains of single mothers, QJE", "Blundell & MaCurdy (1999) \u2013 survey of labor supply elasticities, Handbook of Labor Economics"], "reasoning_summary": "Single mothers are widely documented as having among the highest labor-supply participation elasticities of any demographic group. Classic studies exploiting EITC and welfare reforms (Eissa & Liebman 1996; Meyer & Rosenbaum 2001) find participation elasticities in the range of roughly 0.5\u20131.2, with many estimates clustering around 0.7\u20130.9. Survey articles (Blundell & MaCurdy 1999; Keane 2011) confirm that the participation margin for single mothers is substantially larger than for prime-age men. The medium-run framing allows some adjustment beyond the immediate impact, pushing estimates slightly higher than pure short-run responses. Uncertainty is meaningful because estimates vary across identification strategies, time periods, and exact wage/tax measures, so I place p05 at 0.2 (very conservative, possible if measurement or selection issues are severe) and p95 at 1.5 (plausible upper range from some structural estimates)."}
interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity with respect to the net-of-tax wage for single mothers in the United States.
citations: Eissa & Liebman (1996) – EITC expansions and labor supply of single mothers, QJE
Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) – welfare and tax reforms explaining employment gains of single mothers, QJE
Blundell & MaCurdy (1999) – survey of labor supply elasticities, Handbook of Labor Economics
reasoning_summary: Single mothers are widely documented as having among the highest labor-supply participation elasticities of any demographic group. Classic studies exploiting EITC and welfare reforms (Eissa & Liebman 1996; Meyer & Rosenbaum 2001) find participation elasticities in the range of roughly 0.5–1.2, with many estimates clustering around 0.7–0.9. Survey articles (Blundell & MaCurdy 1999; Keane 2011) confirm that the participation margin for single mothers is substantially larger than for prime-age men. The medium-run framing allows some adjustment beyond the immediate impact, pushing estimates slightly higher than pure short-run responses. Uncertainty is meaningful because estimates vary across identification strategies, time periods, and exact wage/tax measures, so I place p05 at 0.2 (very conservative, possible if measurement or selection issues are severe) and p95 at 1.5 (plausible upper range from some structural estimates).

raw_response: {"interpretation": "Medium-run employment participation elasticity of single mothers in the United States with respect to the net-of-tax wage.", "point_estimate": 0.7, "quantiles": {"p05": 0.2, "p25": 0.45, "p50": 0.7, "p75": 1.0, "p95": 1.5}, "citations": ["Eissa & Liebman (1996), 'Labor Supply Response to the Earned Income Tax Credit', QJE", "Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001), 'Welfare, the Earned Income Tax Credit, and the Labor Supply of Single Mothers', QJE", "Chetty, Guren, Manoli & Weber (2013), 'Does Indivisible Labor Explain the Difference between Micro and Macro Elasticities?', NBER Macroeconomics Annual"], "reasoning_summary": "Single mothers are among the most elastically responding groups in the labor supply literature, largely identified through EITC expansions and welfare reforms in the 1980s\u20131990s. Eissa & Liebman (1996) and Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) are canonical studies finding large participation responses for this group, with implied elasticities broadly in the range of 0.5\u20131.2 depending on specification and time horizon. The medium-run framing pushes estimates somewhat higher than short-run point estimates. A central estimate around 0.7 is consistent with the bulk of the literature, with meaningful uncertainty on both sides\u2014some studies find lower values (~0.3\u20130.5) using more recent data or different identification, while others find values exceeding 1.0. interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity of single mothers in the United States with respect to the net-of-tax wage.
citations: Eissa & Liebman (1996), 'Labor Supply Response to the Earned Income Tax Credit', QJE
Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001), 'Welfare, the Earned Income Tax Credit, and the Labor Supply of Single Mothers', QJE
Chetty, Guren, Manoli & Weber (2013), 'Does Indivisible Labor Explain the Difference between Micro and Macro Elasticities?', NBER Macroeconomics Annual
reasoning_summary: Single mothers are among the most elastically responding groups in the labor supply literature, largely identified through EITC expansions and welfare reforms in the 1980s–1990s. Eissa & Liebman (1996) and Meyer & Rosenbaum (2001) are canonical studies finding large participation responses for this group, with implied elasticities broadly in the range of 0.5–1.2 depending on specification and time horizon. The medium-run framing pushes estimates somewhat higher than short-run point estimates. A central estimate around 0.7 is consistent with the bulk of the literature, with meaningful uncertainty on both sides—some studies find lower values (~0.3–0.5) using more recent data or different identification, while others find values exceeding 1.0. The upper tail extends to ~1.5 reflecting some high-end estimates from structural models.

reasoning_summary: Single mothers are widely documented to have high labor-force participation elasticities relative to other demographic groups, largely because many are at the extensive (participation) margin. Classic EITC studies (Eissa & Liebman 1996; Meyer & Rosenbaum 2001) find participation elasticities in the range of roughly 0.5–1.2 for this group. The extensive-margin elasticity is the dominant component; intensive-margin responses are smaller. Survey reviews place the central tendency around 0.7–0.9 for the extensive margin alone. I center my median at 0.7, reflecting a somewhat conservative read of the literature while acknowledging estimates can run higher (up to ~1.2–1.5) in some studies or policy contexts. The distribution is right-skewed given the possibility of larger estimates in high-wage-change experiments.
